results.explanation: The stage is strongly I/O-bound with low CPU utilization and high I/O time ratio. Write bandwidth is near peak storage bandwidth with 95% utilization, indicating saturation on the write path. Network utilization is very low, ruling out network bottleneck. Large average IO size and high parallelism exclude metadata contention and serialization. Thus, the primary bottleneck is write bandwidth saturation on the storage device.

High parallelism and high bandwidth utilization exclude serialization or lock contention.

The stage is strongly I/O-bound with low CPU utilization and very low bandwidth utilization despite moderate IOPS and very small average I/O size (4 KB). The high number of small writes overwhelms the metadata server on shared NFS storage, causing metadata contention. Network and bandwidth saturation are ruled out due to low network utilization and very low bandwidth use relative to peak.

The stage is strongly I/O-bound with low CPU utilization and very low bandwidth utilization despite high parallelism, indicating the storage bandwidth is not saturated. The workload issues many small I/O operations (avg_io_size_kb=8) with moderate IOPS, typical of metadata overhead. The shared NFS storage and many parallel tasks suggest metadata server contention is limiting performance.

Network and storage bandwidth are underutilized, and the shared NFS storage with many small files leads to metadata contention.

The stage is strongly I/O-bound with very low CPU utilization and extremely low bandwidth utilization despite high parallelism and moderate IOPS. The very small average I/O size (1 KB) combined with low bandwidth and high IOPS indicates many small file operations overwhelming the metadata server. Network and storage bandwidth are not saturated, and data is shared on NFS, which is sensitive to metadata contention.

The stage is heavily I/O bound with low bandwidth utilization despite high parallelism and many concurrent writers to a single shared output file. This strongly indicates multiple tasks are contending for write locks, causing serialization and preventing effective bandwidth usage. Low CPU and network utilization further support that lock contention on shared files is the primary bottleneck.

The stage is strongly I/O-bound with low bandwidth utilization despite high parallelism and many writers per shared file, indicating contention on write locks. The low bw_utilization_ratio despite 128 parallel tasks and 64 writers per shared file suggests multiple tasks are serialized waiting for locks, limiting effective bandwidth. Network and storage bandwidth are not saturated, and the access pattern is large sequential writes, ruling out metadata contention or bandwidth saturation.

The stage is heavily I/O-bound with low bandwidth utilization despite high parallelism and many concurrent writers to a single shared output file on NFS. The low write bandwidth relative to peak and low CPU and network utilization indicate that the bottleneck is contention on write locks for the shared file, preventing effective parallel I/O.

The stage is strongly I/O-bound with high parallelism (64 tasks) but very low bandwidth utilization (0.21) despite a moderate write bandwidth (125 MB/s) far below peak storage bandwidth (600 MB/s). There is only one shared output file with all 64 writers, indicating heavy contention for write locks. CPU utilization is low, and network is not saturated, ruling out compute or network bottlenecks. This pattern strongly suggests lock contention on shared files is limiting effective write throughput.

The stage is strongly I/O-bound with low CPU utilization and low bandwidth utilization despite high parallelism. Many writers (64) are contending on a very small number of shared output files (4), causing lock contention that severely limits effective write bandwidth. This explains the low bw_utilization_ratio despite high parallelism and large aggregate write size.

The stage is strongly I/O-bound with low CPU utilization and low bandwidth utilization despite very high parallelism (400 tasks). The presence of only 2 shared output files with 200 writers each on a shared NFS storage strongly suggests multiple tasks are contending for write locks, causing serialization and low effective bandwidth. Network and storage bandwidth are underutilized, ruling out bandwidth saturation or network bottlenecks.

The stage is strongly I/O-bound with low CPU utilization and low bandwidth utilization despite very high parallelism. This lock contention prevents effective parallel writes, causing low bandwidth utilization and a bottleneck.

The stage is strongly I/O-bound with low CPU utilization, but bandwidth utilization is very low compared to peak storage bandwidth. Despite 100 tasks running in parallel, I/O parallelism is limited to 1 and only one I/O worker is active, indicating serialization of I/O. Network and metadata contention are not issues, so the primary bottleneck is that I/O is forced through a single writer, preventing parallelism.

The stage is I/O-bound with low CPU utilization and low bandwidth utilization despite high parallelism, indicating I/O inefficiency.
